items.q: Does map change the original list?
items.correct: No - in functional programming data is immutable: map returns a NEW list of results and the original list is left untouched.
items.misconception: Yes - map updates each element of the original list in place.
items.random_wrong: Yes - map deletes the original list once it has been read.
items.why: Imperative habits assume in-place update. Immutability is the foundational contract: every operation produces new values, enabling safe composition and easy reasoning.

items.q: Can a pure function read and use a global variable that other code modifies?
items.correct: No - if its result depends on changeable external state, the same input could give different outputs, so the function is no longer pure.
items.misconception: Yes - purity only forbids printing to the screen.
items.random_wrong: Yes - global variables are automatically constants in functional languages.
items.why: Side effects are remembered as outputs only (printing, writing files). Hidden inputs break purity too: referential transparency needs output determined solely by arguments.

items.q: Does filter transform each element of a list?
items.correct: No - filter selects elements, keeping those that satisfy the predicate unchanged; transforming each element is map's job.
items.misconception: Yes - filter changes every element using its function.
items.random_wrong: Yes - filter and map are two names for the same operation.
items.why: Both walk the list with a function, so they blur. The function's role differs: map's returns a new element, filter's returns a yes/no verdict.

items.q: A higher-order function is one that:
items.correct: Takes a function as an argument or returns a function as its result
items.misconception: Always runs at a higher CPU priority
items.why: Correct answer: Takes a function as an argument or returns a function as its result. “Always runs at a higher CPU priority” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Applying map with the function double to the list [1,2,3] produces:
items.why: Correct answer: [2,4,6]. “[1,2,3,1,2,3]” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Only the elements for which the predicate function is true
items.misconception: The list sorted into ascending order
items.random_wrong: The first element of the list
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Only the elements for which the predicate function is true. “The list sorted into ascending order” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Combines all elements of a list into a single value using a combining function
items.misconception: Splits a list into two halves
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Combines all elements of a list into a single value using a combining function. “Splits a list into two halves” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The function map (*2) is applied to the list [1,2,3]. What is the sum of the elements of the resulting list?
items.why: Exact answer: 12. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
